artifacts.content:
Hi Sarah,

I’m on my lunch break hiding in the tiny staff kitchen, so this will be rambly, but I wanted to actually answer your last text instead of just sending heart emojis.

Things here are…okay-ish. Work at Niagara Community Wellness Centre is in that weird phase where they’re hinting about “expanding my role” into more evening events. It would be nice to have the extra money with the rent going up, but I’m nervous about sliding back toward that Fall 2022 mess when I was having panic attacks and the doctor at Niagara Falls Community Health Centre pulled me off work from October to December. I still think about those Wednesday nights at The Grounds Coffeehouse when I did those 2‑hour solo acoustic sets—Dr. Singh literally wrote in my file that playing those mellow covers helped stabilize my sleep more than anything.

Music-wise, I feel like I’m still re-learning how to be a performer. After dropping out of the Brock music degree, I basically disappeared from gigs for a few years and only really started getting back into a regular groove with Daniel in the last year or so. Canal City Acoustic Duo is finally busy enough that I’m having to use a calendar again.

How’s the hospital? Last time we talked you sounded completely fried. I keep worrying about you driving home after those brutal shifts in Hamilton. If you ever want a weekend where we just make pasta, walk the canal, and say nothing, you’re always welcome here.

I’m heading to St. Catharines on Sunday to see Mom and Dad—Mom’s joints have been flaring again, so I’m going to do a batch-cook for their freezer. If you’re off, maybe meet us there?

Love you,
Em

metadata.subject: Quick check-in from the staff kitchen

artifacts.content:
Hi Aunt Lisa,

I’ve been meaning to send a proper update instead of just photos of guitars and farmers’ market sunrises.

Mom’s doing a bit better energy-wise, but I still think about everything she went through in 2022. When we played that Niagara SPCA fundraiser at the St. Catharines Kiwanis Aquatics Centre in May 2023, I don’t know if I ever told you how intense it felt. Daniel and I did a 90‑minute Canal City Acoustic Duo set, and between songs I talked about her breast cancer diagnosis and the chemo months at St. Catharines General—January through September 2022—so people understood why the SPCA’s support meant so much to her. I was shaking more talking than singing.

Fast forward to last March: I ended up back at St. Catharines General with her, this time post-op after her hysterectomy. While she was in recovery, I quietly sang a mini set at her bedside—mostly Sarah McLachlan and Joni Mitchell, super soft acoustic folk‑pop and gentle soft rock. The nurses were sweet about it and even dimmed the lights a bit. That whole experience really cemented that this mellow, mid‑tempo style is where I feel most like myself.

Outside of all the medical stuff, life is pretty ordinary. Mark’s still on weird shifts, so we’re like ships passing some weeks. I’m squirrelling away every extra dollar from duo gigs into a little “maybe someday townhouse” fund. Canal City has a couple of brunch shows coming up, plus the usual Fonthill Farmers’ Market dates once the weather cooperates.

If you’re free next time I’m in St. Catharines, maybe we can grab coffee at The Grounds and I’ll play you one of the new originals.

Love,
Emily

metadata.subject: Mom, music, and a small life update

artifacts.content:
Personal journal – late night thoughts

It’s wild looking back at the last few years of music and realizing how much of it is tied up with church basements and credit card statements.

From spring 2019 through summer 2021, Daniel and I were at St. Andrew’s Vietnamese Catholic Church in Welland twice a month, doing acoustic worship sets. They gave us about $75 per service as an honorarium, which quietly kept me afloat. That money is what let me stay on the payment plan with Meridian Credit Union after I fell behind on Brock University tuition in 2018. I’d pay the card, then still feel okay saying yes to unpaid community things like those early Jammin’ at the Market pilot days.

I don’t usually talk about the church gigs when people ask about Canal City Acoustic Duo, but honestly, that’s where I learned how to keep the volume low, read a room, and transition songs without awkward pauses.

The flip side is that most of what I’ve done has been in tiny indoor spaces—cafés, wineries, church halls—where people are mostly chatting and we’re just the background soundtrack. I’ve barely had to think about “working” a big crowd or holding a proper concert audience’s attention, which still makes me nervous when someone mentions festivals.

Now my weeks are this strange mix of scheduling seniors’ fitness fairs at Niagara Community Wellness Centre, biking home to stir‑fry with Mark, then heading to Daniel’s townhouse to run through Fleetwood Mac and Tracy Chapman arrangements. Feels small, but in a good way.

metadata.title: Journal – Church gigs, money, and tiny rooms

artifacts.content:
Daniel 🟢

Emily: Hey, quick brain dump while I’m on the couch with tea.

Emily: Work at Niagara Community Wellness Centre just dropped another “could you help with this evening workshop?” on me for next month. Between that and Karen’s addiction recovery/trauma group on Wednesday evenings, my weeks are filling up faster than I’d like.

Emily: You know those acoustic folk‑pop sets I’ve been doing for her group since Fall 2023? It’s basically the same stripped‑down Canal City vibe—soft stuff that won’t overwhelm anyone. I actually really like those nights, but they do eat up energy.

Daniel: Yeah, I figured they might. How’s your voice holding up?

Emily: Mostly okay, but I’m trying not to overbook. Honestly, because the day job is events and comms, I have to be picky. When things get busy at the centre, I can’t just say yes to every duo gig or we’d be cancelling all the time, so I’ve kept our calendar pretty low‑key and occasional.

Emily: Re: bookings—got an email for a Saturday night winery show in June. Part of me wants to say yes, but after that February 2020 bar incident in downtown St. Catharines (the drunk guy grabbing me during the Fleetwood Mac cover, the NRPS station visit the next morning with Sarah), I’m still wary of late-night, boozy rooms.

Daniel: I get it. Maybe we stick to markets and brunches.

Emily: Exactly. Daytime Fonthill Farmers’ Market forever. 😂 I’ll send you the tentative dates tonight so we can lock in what we’re comfortable with.

Voice & life reflection – saved in Notes app

I keep circling back to February 2020 like it’s a movie I can’t stop replaying. Solo gig at that downtown St. Catharines bar, mid‑set doing a Fleetwood Mac cover, and then that drunk guy grabbed me out of nowhere. The way my stomach dropped is still in my body. The next morning, Sarah drove in from Hamilton and sat with me at the St. Catharines NRPS station while I filed the report. I don’t think I would’ve gone through with it without her.

From March through August 2020, I basically went underground—no live shows, just practicing at home and at Daniel’s apartment in Welland. We’d run quiet sets in his living room, working on harmonies and transitions, trying to convince my nervous system that music could still feel safe. When I finally eased back into public gigs, it was all daytime stuff: Fonthill Farmers’ Market, canal‑side pop‑ups, Sunday brunches. I still don’t do late‑night downtown bars, and I’m okay with that.

Funny how that lines up with how my genre has evolved. Between Karen’s Wednesday night addiction recovery/trauma group at Niagara Community Wellness Centre and those grief support circles I played for in April–June 2023 after our long‑time client died by suicide, my default now is gentle, mid‑tempo acoustic folk‑pop and soft rock. Songs that sit in the background but still hold people.

Note to self: bring this up with the ENT in St. Catharines in March 2026—not the trauma stuff, but how my voice reacts after those emotionally heavy sets.

metadata.title: Note – Why I avoid late-night bar gigs now

artifacts.content:
Event: Grief Support Circle – background music
Date: Thursday, 2023-05-11
Time: 6:30 p.m. – 8:15 p.m.
Location: Niagara Community Wellness Centre – Group Room B
Attendees: clients + staff (me providing music)

Description/Notes:

Arrive by 6:00 p.m. to help set up chairs in a circle and check in with my manager about the plan for the evening. This is one of the spring grief support sessions we started running April–June 2023 after our long‑time client died by suicide. My role is to provide very gentle, mid‑tempo acoustic folk‑pop / soft rock in the background—mostly fingerpicked covers and a couple of originals.

Song ideas: Norah Jones, Tracy Chapman, softer Fleetwood Mac, maybe one Joni Mitchell if it feels right. Keep the volume low enough that people can talk without raising their voices. Bring the Baby Taylor, capo, simple pedal tuner, no big PA—just the small Bose S1 Pro.

Remember: this is not a performance. Follow the counsellors’ lead. If the energy is too heavy, drop the more lyrical songs and lean into simple, repetitive progressions. Make eye contact only if people seek it out.

Also: I’ve noticed since Fall 2022 that doing these kinds of sets actually calms my own anxiety, similar to those 2‑hour Wednesday night solo shows at The Grounds Coffeehouse that Dr. Singh encouraged when I was on leave from October to December. Have water nearby, do a few quiet vocal warmups in the staff washroom beforehand, and check in with myself on the bike ride home.

Hi Mark,

You’re probably still on that service call, but I wanted to write this down before I forget half of it.

Tonight at your mom’s place in Thorold felt strangely holy in a small way. Since we moved in with Anna back in January after your layoff, the three of us have fallen into this routine: late dinner, dishes, then I sit in the living room with the acoustic and play super quiet sets until she gets sleepy. She’s been so open about the insomnia and panic attacks, and it feels like the only thing that really settles her is these little acoustic folk‑pop / singer‑songwriter runs of originals and soft covers.

I realized mid‑song tonight that this is basically the core of my genre now. The same mellow vibe I used at St. Catharines General last March when I did that bedside mini‑concert of Sarah McLachlan and Joni Mitchell for my mom while she was in post‑op after the hysterectomy, and the same energy I bring into Karen’s Wednesday night trauma group at Niagara Community Wellness Centre. Different rooms—hospital ward, community centre, Anna’s living room—but the same quiet, steady pulse.

Anna asked about the townhouse dream again. I told her we’re still putting every extra Canal City Acoustic Duo cheque into that little savings account, even if it feels like pennies against Niagara prices. She said having us there for these three months has helped more than any pill.

Anyway, just wanted you to know I see how much you’re carrying between work stress and worrying about her. We’ll figure the housing thing out slowly.

Love,
Em

metadata.subject: Tonight’s living room “show” for your mom
